text:
Pens operate on a principle of conservation of writing implements that works in only one direction. The moment a pen enters your hand, it begins quietly applying for a transfer to someone else's pocket, a couch cushion, or that mysterious dimension behind the refrigerator. You are never the owner of a pen; you are merely its current host, and pens are looking for the exit from the day they arrive.

The trouble is that pens are social creatures that have evolved a clever survival strategy: they travel incognito. A single pen is conspicuous and lonely, so it disguises itself as background. You set it down "just for a second" while you answer the door, and in that second it dissolves into the visual noise of your desk, becoming functionally invisible. It is still right there, of course. It is always still right there. But it has switched off its pen-ness, and you will not see it again until you have already borrowed three replacements and bought a fresh ten-pack.

This explains the great paradox of pen ownership, which is that you can simultaneously never have a pen when you need one and own roughly four hundred of them. They are not lost in the sense of being gone. They are in deep cover, scattered across drawers, bags, jacket pockets, and the bottoms of grocery sacks, lying dormant like sleeper agents, waiting for the precise moment when you have given up looking to suddenly reappear in absurd quantities, usually all at once, none of which will write.

There is also the matter of lending, which is where the real magic happens. A borrowed pen never comes back, because the act of lending it grants whoever holds it a kind of unconscious legal title. They are not stealing it. They have simply, in the deepest layer of their brain, reclassified your pen as their pen, and it slides into their pocket with the serene confidence of an object going home. You will do the exact same thing to someone else tomorrow, which is why the global pen supply is in constant migration, like a vast herd quietly drifting from desk to desk, never staying, never quite belonging to anyone, forever just out of reach the moment you actually need to sign something.
